scenario: FEEL regex match matches(s, "abc") is case-sensitive by default. Team wants case-insensitive: matches "ABC", "abc", "Abc". Wonders how. FEEL matches regex case-insensitive flag?
question: FEEL matches regex case-insensitive flag?

options.text: The FEEL matches function does not support any regex flags and always performs case-sensitive matching â€” this approach has limitations. The FEEL matches(input, pattern, flags) function accepts a third optional parameter for regex flags. The flag "i" enables case-insensitive matching. The flag "s" enables single-line mode (dot matches newline). The flag "m" enables multi-line mode (^ and $ match line boundaries). The flag "x" enables extended mode (whitespace ignored). These flags follow the FEEL specification for regular expression matching and are supported in Camunda 8.

md:212-236) is the direct, idiomatic approach. The "i" flag instructs the regex engine to treat uppercase and lowercase as equivalent without needing to transform the input string first. Example: matches("FooBar", "foo", "i") returns true per verbatim doc example :234.

FEEL regex matching via matches() is always case-insensitive by default -- this approach has limitations. md:193-210. Without flags, matches("FooBar", "foo") returns false because the lowercase foo pattern does not match the mixed-case FooBar input. Case-insensitive matching requires explicitly passing the "i" flag as the third argument: matches("FooBar", "foo", "i") returns true (verbatim example :234). Developers relying on the assumption of default case-insensitivity will encounter unexpected false results in production FEEL expressions.

md:212-236: "s" (dot-all, . matches newlines), "m" (multi-line, ^/$ match line breaks), "i" (case insensitive), "x" (comments, ignore whitespace in pattern). Multiple flags combine in one string: "is" for case-insensitive + dot-all. Verbatim example :234: matches("FooBar", "foo", "i") → true. The 2-arg variant matches(input, pattern) at :193-210 is case-sensitive. Flags follow the XPath/XQuery regular expression flag specification as referenced in the DMN standard.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL matches with flags: Signature: matches(input: string, pattern: string, flags: string) → boolean Flags (DMN FEEL spec): "i" (case-insensitive): `feel matches("Hello", "hello", "i") → true matches("Hello", "hello") → false (default case-sensitive) "s" (dot-all): feel matches("line1\nline2", ".+", "s") → true (. matches newline) "m" (multi-line): feel matches("line1\nline2", "^line2", "m") → true (^ matches start of line) "x" (whitespace ignored in pattern): feel matches("abc", "a b c", "x") → true (pattern whitespace ignored) Combined: feel matches(text, "pattern", "is") → case-insensitive + dot-all matches(text, "pattern", "im") → case-insensitive + multi-line Examples: Email validation (case-insensitive domain): feel matches(email, ".+@example\\.com$", "i") Match codes (case-insensitive): feel matches(code, "^[a-z]{3}\\d+$", "i") Compare with contains (per Set 13 Q38): contains(s, substring): case-sensitive by default; no flags. For case-insensitive contains: normalise via lower case. matches offers more flexibility (regex + flags). For multi-pattern OR: feel matches(text, "(option1|option2|option3)", "i") Match any of three options, case-insensitive. Regex syntax: POSIX-style typical in DMN FEEL. Anchors: ^, $. Quantifiers: , +, ?, {n,m}. Classes: [a-z], [^abc], \d, \w, \s. Groups: (...), (?:...). Escaping: Backslash: \\ in FEEL string (string \ literal) escapes regex special char. Special chars to escape: ., , +, ?, (, ), [, ], {, }, |, ^, $, \. Example with escaping: feel matches(version, "^v\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+$") Matches "v1.2.3" format. Best practices: Use flags judiciously: explicit is better than complex patterns. Test regex thoroughly: edge cases. Document regex intent: comment what the pattern checks. Avoid catastrophic backtracking: regex DoS risk on bad patterns. For very complex regex: Use replace (per Set 13 Q37) with regex + replacement. For extraction: regex groups. For the scenario (case-insensitive match): feel matches(input, "abc", "i") Or equivalently: feel matches(lower case(input), "abc") ` Flag approach more idiomatic. Anti-patterns: Over-complex regex: hard to maintain. No flag for case-insensitive when needed: explicit. Regex injection: don't build patterns from user input without escaping.
